MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-25-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. JAMES MOORE, Esq. of Law Office of James Moore, Inc. (tel: 530-885-6286) for Plaintiff.
Atty. STEPHANIE FREISMUTH, Esq. of Clark Hill LLP (tel: 213-891-9100) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANTS JOSEPH TALMADGE, ARLENE TALMADGE, JOSEPH TALMADGE, BEVIN LEW, ALEXIS GARROD, CF COLLECTIVE, HOPPER LIFE LLC, AND TALMADGE & TALMADGE, INC.'s MOTION TO STRIKE 3RD Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants move to strike portions of plaintiff Jason Cook's Third Amended Complaint (TAC). For the reasons stated herein, the motion is granted in part and denied in part.

Defendants seek to strike the prayer for punitive damages in paragraph 127 as to the Seventh Cause of Action for unjust enrichment. The court grants this motion. Unjust enrichment is a quasi-contract theory of recovery. Punitive damages may not be recovered on a breach of contract; it follows that they cannot be recovered on the equitable remedy that supplies like recovery where a contract is unenforceable.

Defendants seek to strike the prayer for punitive damages in paragraph 184 as to the intentional infliction of emotional distress (IIED) cause of action. The court denies this motion; for the reasons stated in the companion order overruling this demurrer, whether Defendants' conduct as alleged in the TAC was malicious and despicable presents a jury question. Cook has pleaded that conduct with sufficient particularity.

Defendants move to strike material added to the TAC that did not appear in the second amended complaint. The court previously granted Cook leave to amend; the factual material that he added arguably supports the IIED cause of action even where it appears in other causes of action and was not beyond the scope of the court's prior order. (See Thomas v. Regents of University of California (2023) 97 Cal.App.5th 587, 605 "If the complaint states a cause of action under any theory, regardless of the title under which the factual basis for relief is stated, that aspect of the complaint is good against a demurrer." [internal quotation marks and citation omitted].)

Defendants also contend that Cook's pleading is inconsistent as to dates of his termination, both internally in the TAC and in comparison to the second amended complaint. The sham pleading doctrine prohibits a party from pleading around facts it has previously pleaded and thereby judicially admitted, but in view of the liberal construction of pleadings and the absence of any contention that the variation in dates affects the viability of Cook's claims (such as a statute of limitations issue), the court declines to parse the pleading so finely. Discovery will flesh out the dates of events pled.

Order submitted.

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument modifies and adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. ERIK CHRISTENSEN, Esq. of Gold Summit Legal, Inc. (tel: 415-516-2760) appearing along with Atty. JAMES MOORE, Esq. of Law Office of James K. More, Inc. (tel: 530-885-6286) for Plaintiff.
Atty. STEPHANIE FREISMUTH, Esq. and Atty. PENELOPE DEIHL, Esq. of Clark Hill, LLP (tel: 213-891-9100) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF JASON COOK's Plaintiff's Motion For Immediate Stay Of Defendants' 16 Sets Of Written Discovery And For A Protective Order; Motion For Immediate Stay And Appointment Of A Discovery Referee In The Alternative.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Jason Cook seeks a stay of defendants' written discovery and a protective order; in the alternative, he seeks appointment of a discovery referee. Defendants oppose all of the requested relief.

The court grants the motion in part. The court finds that appointment of a referee to handle all discovery disputes among the parties is warranted in this case. The referee will be authorized to set the date, time, and place for all hearings; direct the issuance of subpoenas; preside over hearings; take evidence; and rule on objections, motions, and other requests during the course of the hearing.

The court finds that exceptional circumstances exist in this case justifying appointment of the referee. Specifically, there is a great deal of discovery at issue in the present motions; the court has adjudicated discovery motions already; lengthy meet and confer efforts do not seem to advance discovery in the self-executing manner that the Discovery Act intends; and plaintiff's motion for stay of discovery and protective order points to hundreds of individual requests at issue in this motion. In short, the court finds that the discovery issues presented by the parties pose require an "inordinately time-consuming" endeavor by the court. (See Taggares v. Superior Court (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 94, 105.)

Plaintiff requests that the court appoint attorney Philip S. Ward, who has previously acted as a judge pro tem in connection with discovery in this case. Plaintiff also requests that the defendants be ordered to pay the entirety of the cost. The court instead orders the parties to meet and confer about the identity of a mutually acceptable referee, hourly rate, and extent to which court facilities or staff need to be used. (CRC 3.922(b), (f), and (g).) The parties are also ordered to meet and confer about "a fair and reasonable apportionment of reference costs." (CRC 3.922(f)(3).) The apportionment can be based on the parties' ability to pay, but the court has identified no authority permitting it to require defendants to pay referee costs as a sanction, as Plaintiff appears to contend.

The discovery motions presently at issue in this case are continued to March 12, 2025 to allow the parties to reach resolution of the above issues. The parties are directed to inform the court if they reach agreement in advance of the hearing. If the parties cannot, then each party shall file a supplemental brief by March 5, 2025 (with a courtesy copy to the court) listing three proposed referees with the information required by CRC 3.922(b) for each. If any party contends that it is unable to pay a pro rata share of the referee's fees, its March 5, 2025 brief shall also address its ability to pay.

Discovery deadlines in the outstanding requests by defendants that are the subject of Cook's motion for protective order are tolled through March 12, 2025.

Order submitted.
